definition: Order, duty, law, or right conduct; the exact sense depends on context.
Action, deed, or the consequences bound to action.
Discipline, practice, integration, or path toward steadiness and liberation. Literal meaning is "yoking", "uniting", "joining" (from the root yuj: "to yoke", "to unite").
True Self, Soul, or inner principle; kept distinct from ordinary ego or individual personality.
The ultimate reality or absolute principle.
A member of the priestly or learned class; Sanskrit brāhmaṇa.
A quality or constituent tendency of prakriti: sattva, rajas, and tamas.
The guna associated with clarity, balance, and illumination.
The guna associated with activity, passion, impulse, and restlessness.
The guna associated with inertia, dullness, confusion, and obstruction.
Nature, material process, or the field of change and manifestation.
Person, spirit, or conscious principle; meaning varies by passage.
The Supreme Person: epithet of Krishna indicating the supreme reality that transcends both the perishable and the imperishable
Field, especially the body or field of experience in Chapter 13.
Knower of the field; the conscious principle related to kshetra.
Power of appearance, concealment, or manifestation; context-dependent.
Sacrifice, ritual offering, or consecrated action.
Extinction, peace, or release; here often linked with Brahman.
The ongoing cycle of worldly becoming, birth, death, and attachment.
A path or framework of discriminative knowledge; here not always a formal school label.
Ancient sacred texts of the Vedic tradition.
Sacred syllable used as a concise sign of the absolute or divine.
Member of the warrior or ruling class.
Member of the class associated with trade, agriculture, and herding.
Member of the service class in the traditional varna scheme.
Krishna, speaker of the teaching to Arjuna.
Arjuna, the warrior addressed by Krishna.
Epithet of Arjuna: son of Pritha/Kunti.
Epithet of Arjuna: son of Kunti.
Epithet of Arjuna, often understood as winner of wealth.
Epithet of Arjuna, commonly linked with mastery over sleep.
A dynastic or ancestral name, often used as an address.
One of the sons or descendants of Pandu; often refers to Arjuna and his brothers.
Epithet of Krishna.
Epithet of Krishna.
Epithet of Krishna: slayer of Madhu.
Epithet of Krishna.
Epithet of Krishna, often understood as lord of the senses.
Name or epithet of Krishna, son of Vasudeva.
Epithet of Krishna.
Epithet of Krishna, often glossed as the unfailing one.
The blind king of the Kurus, father of the Kauravas; he opens the dialogue by asking Sanjaya what happened at Kurukshetra.
Narrator of the dialogue to Dhritarashtra.
Leader of the Kauravas and son of Dhritarashtra.
Elder of the Kuru dynasty and leading warrior on the Kaurava side.
Teacher of the Kuru princes and warrior on the Kaurava side.
The Kuru lineage or its members; context may refer to the wider dynasty or the Kaurava side.
The battlefield where the dialogue takes place.
One of the Pandavas, brother of Arjuna.
Warrior allied with Duryodhana on the Kaurava side.
King of Panchala, father of Draupadi and Dhrishtadyumna.
Wife of the Pandavas.
Solar deity to whom Krishna says he first taught this yoga.
Ancient progenitor or lawgiver who receives the teaching from Vivasvan.
Ancient king who receives the teaching from Manu.
The creator god; distinct from Brahman, the ultimate reality.
Honorific title for the Lord or Blessed One, used for Krishna.
Epithet of Krishna or Vishnu.
A divine name associated with Krishna in the text.
Lord of creatures; a creator figure.
Vedic deity associated with cosmic order and waters.
Deity associated with death and restraint.
Sacred formula meaning 'That', part of the expression Om Tat Sat.
Being, truth, or goodness; in 17.23 part of the sacred formula Om Tat Sat.
The end or culmination of the Vedas; in the text, the knowledge taught by the Vedic tradition.
